q: "To cherish and follow the noble ideas which inspired our national struggle for freedom" is a provision of which one of the following of the Constitution of India?
o: Directive principles of the state policy
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: Fundamental Duties Explanation: The Fundamental Duties are set out in Article 51-A of the Constitution which remind every citizen to observe certain basic norms and democratic conduct in the country. The Fundamental Duties were included in the Constitution with the idea of curbing subversive and unconstitutional activities. Originally, there were no fundamental duties in the Constitution The 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act (1976) included ten Fundamental Duties. According to Article 51A, it shall be the duty of every citizen of India: to abide by the Constitution and respect its ideals and institutions, the National Flag and the National Anthem to cherish and follow the noble ideals that inspired the national struggle for freedom to uphold and protect the sovereignty, unity and integrity of India to defend the country and render national service when called upon to do so to promote harmony and the spirit of common brotherhood amongst all the people of India transcending religious, linguistic and regional or sectional diversities and to renounce practices derogatory to the dignity of women to value and preserve the rich heritage of the country’s composite culture to protect and improve the natural environment including forests, lakes, rivers and wildlife and to have compassion for living creature to develop scientific temper, humanism and the spirit of inquiry and reform to safeguard public property and to abjure violence to strive towards excellence in all spheres of individual and collective activity so that the nation constantly rises to higher levels of endeavour and achievement to provide opportunities for education to his child or ward between the age of six and fourteen years. This duty was added by the 86th Constitutional Amendment Act, 2002. Hence, the correct answer is b.

q: Which one of the following does NOT fall under the definition of the Money Bill?
o: Amendment of law with respect to any financial obligations undertaken by the Government of India
The payment of money into the Consolidated Fund of India
Any financial bill as per requirements of Article 117
Appropriation of money out of the Consolidated Fund of India
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: Union Parliament Explanation: A Money Bill, as defined in Article 110 of the Indian Constitution, includes provisions related to matters such as the imposition, abolition, remission, alteration, or regulation of any tax; the regulation of borrowing by the government; the custody of the Consolidated Fund or the Contingency Fund of India; and the payment of moneys into or the withdrawal of moneys from any such fund. While Article 117 provides for the procedure for passing of financial bills, it does not necessarily classify all financial bills as Money Bills. Only bills containing exclusively provisions as specified in Article 110 are considered Money Bills. Hence, the correct answer is c.

q: Which one among the following is NOT the central feature of Polyarchy?
o: Government is in the hands of elected officials
Right to run for office is restricted
Practically all adults have the right to vote
There is free expression and a right to criticize and protest
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: Political Science Concepts Explanation: According to Robert Dahl's concept of polyarchy, the central features are: Government is in the hands of elected officials. Practically all adults have the right to vote. There is free expression and a right to criticize and protest. The right to run for office is not restricted in a polyarchy. Dahl emphasizes "universal suffrage and the right to run for public office" as a necessary institution for polyarchy to function adequately. Therefore, the correct answer is b.

q: Which one of the following statements regarding the Directive Principles of State Policy is NOT correct?
o: State shall follow the Directive Principles of State Policy both in the matter of administration as well as in the making of laws
The Directive Principles of State Policy embody the object of the State under the republican Constitution
The Directive Principles of State Policy have precedence over the Fundamental Rights in case of conflict between the two
The Directive Principles of State Policy are not enforceable in the Courts
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: Directive Principles of State Policy Explanation: The Directive Principles of State Policy are enumerated in Part IV of the Constitution from Articles 36 to 51. The Directive Principles of State Policy are the principles that are fundamental in the governance of the country and it shall be the duty of the State to apply these principles in making laws. The Directive Principles of State Policy embody the object of the State under the republican Constitution. The Directive Principles of State Policy are not legally enforceable in a court of law. The Directive Principles of State Policy provide a framework and guidance for the formulation of laws and policies by the state. The Directive Principles of State Policy cannot override all the fundamental rights. Directive Principles of State Policy does not have precedence over Fundamental Rights. Hence, the correct answer is c.

q: Which of the following statements about Max Weber's ideal theory of Bureaucracy is/are correct ? 1. It refers to the dominant class character. 2. Bureaucracy is associated with structural and behavioral characteristics. Select the correct answer using the code given below :
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: Political Science Concepts Explanation: Max Weber's theory of bureaucracy is a fundamental concept in management theory. Weber, a German sociologist, emphasized the importance of bureaucratic management for efficient organizational functioning. Max Weber's ideal theory of Bureaucracy does not refer to the dominant class character. Bureaucracy is indeed associated with structural and behavioral characteristics according to Max Weber's theory. Hence, the correct answer is b.

q: Which one of the following statements about the Attorney-General of India is NOT correct?
o: He shall have the right to speak in the Houses of Parliament
He shall have a right of audience in all Courts in the territory of India in the performance of his official duties
He must have the same qualifications as are required to be a Judge of the Supreme Court
He is a whole-time counsel for the Government
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: Attorney General Explanation: Article 76 has provided the office of Attorney General. He is appointed by the President and he is the highest law officer in the country. He must be: Must be a person who is qualified to be a Supreme Court Judge,i.e Must have been a judge of some High Court for 5 years or an Advocate of the High Court for 10 years. Term of office is not fixed by the Constitution. No ground of removal is provided in the Constitution. Holds office during the Pleasure of the President. He has not been debarred from Private Practice. However, the Attorney General is not a fulltime counsel for the Government. He does not fall in the category of government servants. Further, he is not debarred from private legal practice. Hence, the correct answer is d.

q: Which of the following pairs of list and contents is/are correctly matched? List Subject 1. State list Public health and sanitation 2. Union list Citizenship, naturalisation and aliens 3. Concurrent Legal, medical list and other professions Select the correct answer using the code:
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: Centre-State Relations Explanation: The Constitution provides for a three-fold distribution of legislative subjects between the Centre and the states. It includes List-I (the Union List), List-II (the State List) and List-III (the Concurrent List) in the Seventh Schedule. The Parliament has exclusive powers to make laws with respect to any of the matters enumerated in the Union List. | | Defence This list has at present 98 subjects (originally 97 subjects) like citizenship, naturalisation and aliens, defence, banking, foreign affairs, currency, atomic energy, insurance, communication, inter- state trade and commerce, census, audit and so on. The state legislature has “in normal circumstances” exclusive powers to make laws with respect to any of the matters enumerated in the State List. This has at present 59 subjects (originally 66 subjects) like public order, police, public health and sanitation, agriculture, prisons, local government, fisheries, markets, theaters, gambling and so on. Both, the Parliament and state legislature can make laws with respect to any of the matters enumerated in the Concurrent List. This list has at present 52 subjects (originally 473 subjects) like Legal, medical and other professions, criminal law and procedure, civil procedure, marriage and divorce, population control and family planning, electricity, labour welfare, economic and social planning, drugs, newspapers, books and printing press, and others. Hence the correct answer is b.

q: Which one of the following is not correct with reference to the Tenth schedule of the Constitution?
o: The schedule lays down that elected members of the Parliament and State Legislatures may be disqualified on the grounds of defection
Disqualification on ground of defection does not apply in cases of merger with another political party
Cases of dispute are decided by the speaker or Chairman of the House concerned
The Supreme Court of India is the final arbiter in cases which remain unresolved
subject: polity
exp: Key concept : Anti- defection law Explanation: Anti defection law was legislated by the Parliament and inserted into the constitution under Tenth schedule by 52 nd constitutional amendment act Anti defection law prevents the legislator to change his party It allowed defection if ⅓ of the party is defecting But by 91 st constitutional amendment it was changed to ⅔ of the party strength A member will attract disqualification if : He voluntarily gives up the membership of the party on whose membership it was elected He votes or abstains from the voting contrary to direction of his party ⅓ of the party member or less, joins the new party Independently elected member joins the new party As per the 1985 Act, a 'defection' by one- third of the elected members of a political party was considered a 'merger'. But the 91st Constitutional Amendment Act, 2003, changed this and now at least two-thirds of the members of a party must be in favour of a "merger" for it to have validity in the eyes of the law. The question of disqualification under the Tenth Schedule is decided by the Chairman, in the case of legislative council and, Speaker, in the case of legislative assembly (and not by the governor). In 1992, the Supreme Court ruled that the decision of Chairman/Speaker in this regard is subject to judicial review. Hence, the correct answer is b.

q: Which of the following are the defining features of liberal democracy? 1. Constitutional government based on formal, usually legal, rules. 2. Guarantees of civil liberties and individual rights. 3. It invests political authority with potentially unlimited power. Select the correct answer using the code given below :
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: Political Science Concepts Explanation: In a liberal democracy, the government is chosen (elected) as per the rules and law of the land. Liberal democracies are based on the principle of elected representatives who are chosen by the citizens through regular, free, and fair elections. These representatives are responsible for making laws and governing on behalf of the people. The government is subject to the rule of law, meaning that everyone is accountable under the law. Laws are applied equally to all citizens, and no one is above the law. Individual rights are given prior importance. Liberal democracies prioritize the protection of individual rights and freedoms, including freedom of speech, religion, press, assembly, and association, as well as rights to privacy, property, and due process. Hence, the correct answer is b.

q: Which one of the following authorities CANNOT organize 'Lok Adalats'?
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: Lok Adalat Explanation: Lok Adalat, which means “People’s Court“, is a unique alternative dispute resolution mechanism in India aimed at providing speedy and inexpensive justice to litigants. A Lok Adalat shall have jurisdiction to determine and to arrive at a compromise or settlement between the parties to a dispute in respect of : any case pending before any court; or any matter that falls within the jurisdiction of any court and is not brought before such court. Thus, the Lok Adalat can deal with not only the cases pending before a court but also with the disputes at the pre-litigation stage. But, the Lok Adalat shall have no jurisdiction in respect of any case or matter relating to an offense not compoundable under any law. In other words, the offences that are non- compoundable under any law fall outside the purview of the Lok Adalat. Lok Adalats are organized by the District Authority, Taluk Legal Services Committee, and High Court Legal Services Committee. Panchayat Committees do not have the authority to organize Lok Adalats. Hence, the correct answer is a.

q: Consider the following statements relating to short notice questions asked in the Legislature: 1. These relate to matters of urgent public importance and can be asked for oral answer at a notice less than 10 days. 2. Short notice questions can be admissible if the Minister concerned agrees to answer to it. 3. Short notice questions are asked during question hour. Which of the statements given above are correct?
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: Parliamentary Proceedings Explanation: The first hour of every parliamentary sitting is allocated to Question Hour. During this time, members ask questions, and ministers provide answers. | | Defence There are three types of questions: starred, unstarred, and short notice. Starred questions require an oral answer and allow for supplementary questions. Unstarred questions require a written answer and do not permit supplementary questions. Short notice questions are asked with less than ten days' notice and are answered orally. Private members can also be addressed with questions related to bills, resolutions, or other House business they are responsible for. The procedure for questioning private members is the same as that for questioning ministers. Short notice questions can be admissible even if the Minister concerned does not agree to answer them. They are not asked specifically during question hour. They can be asked at any time during the proceedings of the Legislature, as long as they fulfill the criteria of being related to urgent public importance. Hence, the correct answer is b.

q: The Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail (VVPAT) system was used for the first time by the Election Commission of India in
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: Elections Explanation: The Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail (VVPAT) system is a method used in elections to provide an additional layer of transparency and accountability. It allows voters to verify their vote by providing a paper trail that can be electronically recorded and stored. Voter Verifiable Paper Audit Trail (VVPAT) is used along with Electronic Voting Machines (EVM) during the time of elections. VVPAT is a system of using EVM to provide feedback to voters. This system helps the voters to verify that their vote was casted correctly by providing a slip in which the name of the voter along with the symbol he has voted for is printed out The VVPAT system was used for the first time by the Election Commission of India in Noksen Assembly Constituency in Nagaland. Hence, the correct answer is b.

q: Which of the following is NOT provided for under the Directive Principles of State Policy?
o: Right to work, to education and to public assistance in certain cases
Uniform Civil Code for the citizens
Participation of workers in the management of local-self government
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: Directive principles of State Policy Explanation: Article 41 emphasizes the responsibility of the State to provide for its citizens in various aspects such as employment, education, and public assistance in cases of need. Article 43A - To take steps to secure the participation of workers in the management of industries Article 44 of the Constitution of India directs the state to strive towards implementing a uniform civil code for all citizens, regardless of their religion, to replace personal laws based on religious scriptures and customs governing various communities. Article 50 states that- "The State shall take steps to separate the judiciary from the executive in the public services of the State." However, there is no provision for Participation of workers in the management of local-self government. Hence, the correct answer is d.

q: Which one of the following is NOT a correct feature of a Money Bill?
o: It can be introduced only in the Lok Sabha
A Deputy Speaker can also certify a Money Bill in case of office of Speaker falling vacant
A Speaker's authority is final as to whether a bill is Money Bill or not
It can be referred to a joint committee of the two Houses
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Union government Explanation: A money bill can be introduced in Lok sabha but not in Rajya Sabha. Rajya Sabha cannot reject or amend a Money Bill. It cannot be referred to joint committee of the two houses. The Speaker of the Lok Sabha determines whether a Bill is a Money Bill, and their decision is final. Article 110 of the Constitution deals with Money Bills in India. A Deputy Speaker can also certify a Money Bill in case of the office of the Speaker falling vacant. A Money Bill must be recommended by the President and can only be introduced in Lok Sabha. It requires a simple majority to pass in Lok Sabha. Rajya Sabha may offer recommendations, but Lok Sabha can reject them. If no recommendations are given within 14 days, the Bill is deemed passed by Parliament. A Bill is classified as a Money Bill if it pertains solely to taxation, borrowing of money by the government, or expenditure from the Consolidated Fund of India. Money bills apply to matters such as the imposition, abolition, remission, alteration, or regulation of any tax by any local authority or body for local purposes. Hence, the correct option is d.

q: Which of the following statement(s) regarding passing a law under the State List is/are correct? 1. Even in the sphere (State List) exclusively reserved for the States, Parliament can legislate under certain circumstances 2. Parliament cannot legislate a subject under the State List under any circumstances 3. A resolution supported by tw-othirds of the members present and voting is required to be passed by Rajya Sabha to pass a law under the State List Select the correct answer using the code given below :
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Centre-State Relations Explanation: Article 249 of the Indian Constitution stipulates that if the Council of States (Rajya Sabha) passes a resolution with the support of not less than two-thirds of the members present and voting, declaring it necessary or expedient in the national interest. Parliament may legislate on matters enumerated in the State List specified in the resolution. Parliament can legislate on subjects listed in the State List under certain circumstances: During a national emergency as per Article 250. When a state requests Parliament to do so under Article 252. To implement international agreements under Article 253. During President's Rule under Article 356. Hence, the correct option is c.

q: According to the Delimitation Commission of India and subject to Article 333 of the Constitution of India, what is the maximum number of members that a State Legislative Assembly may have?
subject: polity
exp: Key concept:State legislatures Explanation: Article 170 mandates the presence of a Legislative Assembly in every Indian state. The size of these assemblies is regulated by Article 333 of the Constitution. Each state's Legislative Assembly can have between 60 and 500 constituencies, with members elected directly. Article 172 stipulates that the tenure of a Legislative Assembly is five years, beginning from its inaugural meeting. Article 171 governs the composition of the Legislative Council, limiting its size to one-third of the Legislative Assembly's total membership. Hence, the correct option is b.

q: The Governor of a state may exercise discretionary powers when 1. he or she is not convinced by the advice of the Council of Ministers 2. the Constitution requires him/her to act in his/her discretion in certain matters 3. he or she is appointed as the administrator of an adjoining Union Territory Select the correct answer using the code given below :
subject: polity
exp: Key concept:State government Explanation: The Governor of a state exercises discretionary powers in various situations: When the Constitution mandates acting at their discretion in certain matters. When reserving a bill for the President's consideration without the advice of the Council of Ministers. When recommending President's rule in the state. When holding additional charge as administrator of a Union Territory. When determining royalties for mineral exploration in certain states. When seeking information from the Chief Minister on administrative and legislative affairs. Situational discretion is exercised in: Appointing a Chief Minister when no party has a clear majority or in case of the incumbent's death. Dismissing the council of ministers for failing to prove confidence. Dissolving the state legislative assembly when it loses its majority. Hence, the correct option is a.

q: Which one of the following is NOT true of socialism?
o: Socialists analyse society in terms of income distribution
It emphasises the centrality of community
Socialists believe that class divisions in society are a fact and therefore irremediable
It believes that material benefits should be distributed on the basis of need
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Socialism Explanation: Socialism encompasses economic and social systems. It is characterized by communal ownership and democratic control of means of production. It emphasizes humans as interconnected by their shared humanity not the centrality of community Differs from communism, which abolishes private ownership entirely, and capitalism, which emphasizes total private ownership. Socialists basically believe that class divisions in society are a fact and therefore irremediable and analyse society in terms of income distribution Advocates for limited private ownership. Balances centralized government control and market forces. Aims to meet societal needs without creating rigid class distinctions. It believes that material benefits should be distributed on the basis of need. Hence, the correct option is b.

q: In relation to the fundamental duties enshrined in the Constitution of India, which one of the following is NOT correct ?
o: The fundamental duties were not originally a part of the Constitution
To safeguard public property and abjure violence are fundamental duties
They are not enforceable through writs
They may not be used for determining constitutionality of laws
subject: polity
exp: Key concept:Fundamental duties Explanation: Fundamental Duties can be used by courts to evaluate the constitutionality of laws. Laws that uphold fundamental duties are considered reasonable in court challenges. Originally absent from the constitution, Fundamental Duties were added via the 42nd Constitutional Amendment Act of 1976. They are listed under Part IV A of the constitution. To safeguard public property and abjure violence are fundamental duties. They are not enforceable through writs. Initially, 10 Fundamental Duties were included, with the 11th added later through the 86th constitutional amendment act of 2002. Article 51A outlines the Fundamental duties of every Indian citizen. Hence, the correct option is d.

q: In relation to the powers of the Union Government of India, which one of the following statements is NOT correct?
o: The Government of India may undertake legislative functions of a territory outside of India by agreement with the Government of that territory
The Union Government may not entrust a State Government with functions over matters where executive power of the Union extends
The Union Government may direct States for construction and maintenance of means of communication of military importance
The Governor may, with consent of Government of India, entrust its officers with functions on matters which fall within the executive power of the State
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Centre-State Relations Explanation: Article 260 of the Indian constitution allows the Government of India to undertake executive, legislative, or judicial functions of any territory not part of India by agreement with the respective government. Article 257(2) grants the Union executive power to give directions to a State regarding the construction and maintenance of communication means declared as of national or military importance. However, Article 258(1) doesn't allow the President to entrust functions to a State Government or its officers without conditions. Article 258(2) permits Parliament to enact laws that apply to a State even if the State Legislature lacks jurisdiction over the matter, empowering the State or its officers accordingly. Hence,the correct option is b.

q: Which one of the following statements with regard to Censure Motion is NOT correct ?
o: A leave of the House is required to move it
The Government is free to fix time and date for its discussion
It can also be moved against the entirety of the Council of Ministers
The Speaker decides whether the motion is in order
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Union Legislature Explanation: A censure motion expresses strong disapproval or criticism of government policies. It must specify the charges against the government. The Speaker determines whether the motion is in order. No leave of the house is required to move a No-Confidence motion. A motion of Censure, once admitted, must be taken up within ten days of the leave being granted. The Government does not have the authority to fix the schedule for its own censure. If passed, the Government must seek the confidence of the house immediately and provide reasons for its adoption in the Lok Sabha. It can target an individual Minister, a group of Ministers, or the entire Council of Ministers for specific policies and actions. The Council of Ministers does not need to resign if the motion passes in the Lok Sabha. Hence,the correct option is b.

q: Who among the following was the first non- official member to be elected as Speaker of the Central Legislative Assembly on 24-8-1925 ?
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Union Parliament Explanation: Vithalbhai J Patel served as the first elected President of the Central Legislative Assembly in 1925, and he was re-elected in 1927. He played a crucial role in establishing clear rules, regulations, practices, and procedures for the effective functioning of the Assembly. Known for his staunch defense of members' rights and privileges, he successfully advocated for the creation of a separate office for the Legislative Assembly, independent of the Government, in 1928. His portrait, painted by C.N. Row, was unveiled by the then Speaker of the Constituent Assembly (Legislative), Shri G.V Mavalankar, on 8 March 1948. Vithalbhai J Patel was a co-founder of the Swaraj Party and the brother of Sardar Patel. He left the Congress to establish the Swaraj Party alongside Chittaranjan Das and Motilal Nehru. In 1914, Vithalbhai played a significant role in the Bombay provincial legislative council, particularly in advocating for the "Bombay District Municipal Act Amendment Bill" and "The Town Planning Bill." Hence, the correct option is a.

q: The idea of social justice requires that 1. equal treatment be supplemented with principle of proportionality 2. due and equal consideration is given to all individuals 3. equality of treatment be transcended to recognise special needs Select the correct answer using the code given below :
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Social Justice Explanation: Social justice focuses on fairness in societal relations and equal access to wealth, opportunities, and social privileges. Access to resources ensures equal opportunities for different socioeconomic groups, aiming for an equal start in life. Equity provides tailored tools based on individual needs and socioeconomic status to achieve similar outcomes, unlike equality where everyone receives the same tools. Participation allows everyone to voice opinions, concerns, and participate in decision-making processes affecting their lives. Understanding diversity and valuing cultural differences aid policymakers in creating inclusive policies. Human rights are foundational to social justice and are closely intertwined with its principles. Social justice does not require equal treatment be supplemented with principle of proportionality. Due and equal consideration is given to all individuals is required. Also equality of treatment be transcended to recognise special needs. Hence, the correct option is d.

q: Which of the following statements are correct with regard to Speaker/Deputy Speaker, Lok Sabha? 1. On dissolution of the Lok Sabha, both the Speaker and Deputy Speaker cease to be members of the House. 2. Both Speaker and Deputy Speaker vacate their office on dissolution of the Lok Sabha 3. Only Deputy Speaker vacates his office and Speaker shall not vacate his office until immediately before the first meeting of the Lok Sabha after dissolution Select the correct answer using the code given below :
subject: polity
exp: Key concept:Parliament Explanation: On the dissolution of the Lok Sabha, although the Speaker ceases to be a member of the House, they do not vacate their office. Similarly, the Deputy Speaker also ceases to be a member of the House upon dissolution, but unlike the Speaker, they vacate their office. However, the Speaker shall not vacate their office until immediately before the first meeting of the Assembly after the dissolution. This means that both the Speaker and Deputy Speaker cease to be members of the House upon dissolution, but the Speaker retains their office until the first meeting of the Assembly convenes after the dissolution. Hence, the correct option is d.

q: A member giving notice of a resolution for removal of Speaker, Lok Sabha should be addressed to the
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Parliament Explanation: To initiate the removal of the Speaker or Deputy Speaker, a member must submit a written notice to the Secretary- General of Lok Sabha. The notice must be in accordance with Article 94(c) of the Constitution. After receiving the motion, the Speaker schedules it for discussion on a day set after fourteen days from the receipt of the notice. Rule 200A of Lok Sabha lays out conditions for the admissibility of such a resolution: It must specify charges clearly. It should be expressed in a clear and precise manner. It must avoid arguments, inferences, irony, imputations, or defamatory statement. Hence,the correct option is b.

q: Who was the President of the Constituent Assembly?
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Making of the constitution Explanation: The Constituent Assembly convened for the first time in New Delhi on December 9, 1946, and concluded its final session on January 24, 1950. The key appointments made by the Constituent Assembly were as follows: Temporary President: Sachchidananda Sinha President: Rajendra Prasad Vice Presidents: HC Mookerjee and VT Krishnamachari Chairman of Drafting Committee: BR Ambedkar Constitutional Advisor: BN Rau Major Committees of the Constituent Assembly included: Drafting Committee chaired by B. R. Ambedkar Union Power Committee led by Jawaharlal Nehru Union Constitution Committee also chaired by Jawaharlal Nehru Provincial Constitution Committee headed by Vallabhbhai Patel Advisory Committee on Fundamental Rights, Minorities, and Tribal and Excluded Areas, also overseen by Vallabhbhai Patel. Hence, the correct option is b.

q: Which of the following is/are correct in relation to the Panchayats? 1. Only the Parliament may endow Panchayats with powers and authority 2. A state may devolve powers to Panchayats in preparation of plans for economic development and social justice 3. A state may devolve powers to Panchayats in implementation of schemes for economic development and social justice Select the correct answer using the code given below :
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Panchayats and Municipalities Explanation: The state can devolve powers to panchayats for planning economic development and social justice, including areas like water, sanitation, and education. Panchayats may also receive powers for implementing schemes related to economic development and social justice. However, the state legislature is not authorized to authorize panchayats to levy, collect, and appropriate taxes, duties, tolls, and fees, nor can they assign such levies collected by the state government to panchayats. Article 243G of the Constitution outlines the powers, authority, and responsibilities of panchayats, allowing state legislatures to endow them with necessary powers for self-government. The Constitution (73rd Amendment) Act of 1992 introduced Part IX into the Constitution, focusing on panchayats. This amendment provided for 29 subjects under panchayati raj institutions. Hence, the correct option is c.

q: With regard to Panel of Chairperson, Lok Sabha, which of the following statements is/are correct? 1. Panel of Chairperson is drawn from the ruling party only 2. Panel of Chairperson is nominated by different political parties and appointed by Speaker, Lok Sabha 3. Panel of Chairperson consists of 10 members and one of them presides over the House when both Speaker and Deputy Speaker are not there Select the correct answer using the code given below :
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Parliament Explanation: Under the Rules of Lok Sabha, the Speaker nominates from amongst the members a panel of not more than ten chairpersons. These members can act as Chairpersons. They can preside over the House when the Speaker and Deputy Speaker are absent. They do so only upon request from the Speaker or Deputy Speaker. If a member of the panel of chairpersons is absent, the House selects another person to act as Speaker. However, a member of the panel of chairpersons cannot preside over the House if the Speaker or Deputy Speaker position is vacant. Hence, the correct option is c.

q: Which one of the following statements regarding Public Accounts Committee is correct?
o: It consists of fifteen members from Lok Sabha and seven members from Rajya Sabha
It consists of twenty members from Lok Sabha and ten members from Rajya Sabha
The term of members of Public Accounts Committee is five years
Finance Minister is the Chairperson of the Public Accounts Committee
subject: polity
exp: Key concept:Parliament Explanation: The Public Accounts Committee (PAC) comprises a total of 22 members: 15 from Lok Sabha and 7 from Rajya Sabha. Members are elected annually by Parliament using a proportional representation system. The term of office for PAC members is one year. Its primary function is to scrutinize the annual audit reports of the Comptroller and Auditor General of India (CAG), presented to Parliament by the President. The Public Accounts Committee was established in 1921 following its initial mention in the Government of India Act, 1919. Notably, Ministers are ineligible to serve as members of the Public Accounts Committee. Hence, the correct option is a.

q: Which of the following statement(s) regarding promulgation of ordinance is/are correct? 1. President can promulgate ordinances which are of temporary duration 2. An ordinance promulgated by the President has to be ratified by Parliament within six weeks of its re-assembly Select the correct answer using the code given below :
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: President of India Explanation: Article 123 empowers the President to issue ordinances when Parliament is not in session, and these ordinances are temporary in nature. Parliament must approve the ordinance within six weeks of reconvening to give it permanence. Actions completed under the ordinance before it lapses remain valid. Ordinances can only be issued on subjects within the legislative purview of the Indian Parliament. Ordinances have the same legal effect as acts passed by Parliament. Article 213 similarly empowers the Governor to issue ordinances for states, subject to similar conditions and limitations. Hence, the correct option is c.

q: Which of the following statements with regard to the Speaker, Lok Sabha is/are correct? 1. The election of the Speaker of Lok Sabha is conducted by the Election Commission of India 2. The Speaker on assuming his office is not required to make and subscribe oath or affirmation 3. The Speaker does not exercise a casting vote in case of equality of votes Select the correct answer using the code given below :
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Parliament Explanation: Chapter III of Lok Sabha Rules details the procedure for the election of a Speaker. Rule 7(1) stipulates that the President determines the date for the Speaker's election, and the Secretary-General is responsible for informing all members of the House about this date. Rule 7(2) specifies that any member can submit a written motion before noon on the day preceding the fixed date, proposing another member as Speaker. This motion must be addressed to the Secretary-General, seconded by another member, and accompanied by a statement from the proposed member expressing willingness to serve as Speaker. Article 100 provides further insights. It states that the Chairman, Speaker, or their substitutes do not vote initially but can cast a tie-breaking vote if needed. Moreover, it clarifies that while the Speaker assumes office without taking an oath, a Pro tem Speaker must swear an oath or affirmation provided by the President. Hence, the correct option is a.

q: As per provisions of the Constitution of India, which one of the following is correct?
o: Only Parliament has the power to legislate over a subject under the Concurrent List
Both Parliament and State Legislatures have the power to legislate over a subject under the Concurrent List
A legislation made under the Concurrent List by Parliament should be ratified by half of the State Legislatures so as to become a law of the country
Only State Legislatures are empowered to make laws under Concurrent List
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Centre-State Relations Explanation: The Indian constitution divides powers between the central and state governments, outlined in the seventh schedule. The seventh schedule comprises three lists: Union, State, and Concurrent. The Concurrent list features subjects over which both central and state governments possess authority. Subjects like Education (including technical and medical education), population control, criminal law, prevention of cruelty to animals, etc., fall under the Concurrent list. The 42nd Amendment Act of 1976 transferred five subjects from the State List to the Concurrent List, including education, forests, weights and measures, protection of wild animals and birds, and administration of justice, except for the Supreme Court and High Courts. Hence, the correct option is b.

q: The Constitution of India does NOT guarantee which one of the following as a fundamental right?
o: Right to carry on trade or business
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Fundamental Rights Explanation: Originally, the Constitution provided for seven Fundamental Rights namely: 1. Right to equality (Articles 14–18) 2. Right to freedom (Articles 19–22) 3. Right against exploitation (Articles 23– 24) 4. Right to freedom of religion (Articles 25–28) 5. Cultural and educational rights (Articles 29–30) 6. Right to property (Article 31) 7. Right to constitutional remedies (Article 32) However, the right to property was deleted from the list of Fundamental Rights by the 44th Amendment Act, 1978. It is made a legal right under Article 300- A in Part XII of the Constitution. So at present, there are only six Fundamental Rights. Hence, the correct answer is c.

q: Which one of the following fundamental rights has not been provided to a person?
o: Protection against prosecution and punishment for the same offence more than once
To refuse to give his/her sample of handwriting as evidence to support a prosecution against him/her
To act as a witness against himself/herself
Right not to be convicted of any offence except for violation of a law in force at the time of commission of the act charged as an offence
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Fundamental rights Explanation: Article 20 of the Constitution provides protection to accused individuals against arbitrary and excessive punishment. It applies to all individuals, whether citizens, foreigners, or legal entities like corporations. The three main provisions of Article 20 are: No ex-post-facto law: Prevents conviction for an act not considered an offence at the time of its commission. No double jeopardy: Prohibits prosecution or punishment for the same offence more than once. No self-incrimination: Protects individuals from being compelled to testify or witness against themselves. An ex-post-facto law applies penalties retroactively, but Article 20 prohibits this only for criminal laws, not civil or tax laws. This provision restricts conviction or sentencing under ex-post-facto criminal laws, not the trial process itself. The fundamental right that has not been provided to a person is the right to refuse to give his/her sample of handwriting as evidence to support a prosecution against him/her. This right is not explicitly mentioned in the fundamental rights provided by the Indian Constitution. Hence, the correct option is b.

q: Who among the following warned his colleagues that the Constituent Assembly was British made and was "working the British plan as the British should like it to be worked out"?
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Making of the Indian Constitution Explanation: Somnath Lahiri, leader of Communist Party of India, warned his colleagues that the Constituent Assembly was British made and was "working the British plan as the British should like it to be worked out". Lahiri was a member of the Indian Constituent Assembly from Bengal and was known for his socialist views. He made this statement during the debates in the Constituent Assembly, expressing his concern about the British influence on the Assembly's proceedings and the potential for intensified conflicts within the Assembly Hence, the correct answer is a.

q: Which one of the following statements with regard to Maneka Gandhi vs. Union of India case, 1978 is not correct?
o: It was held that Article 19 and Article 21 are not watertight compartments.
It was held that a law coming under Article 21 may not satisfy the requirements of Article 19.
A fair trial eliminates the biases against the accused in the trial.
The right to life under Article 21 does not include the right to die.
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Important Judgments Explanation: In the Maneka Gandhi vs. Union of India case in 1978, the petitioner filed a writ petition under Article 32 of the Constitution. She argued that the seizure of her passport violated her fundamental rights guaranteed by Articles 14, 19, and 21. The judgment emphasized that fundamental rights in Part III of the Constitution are not distinct or mutually exclusive. Any law restricting personal liberty must adhere to one or more fundamental rights under Article 19. Article 14's concept of reasonableness must be applied to ensure fair and just procedures. Although Article 21 mentions "procedure established by law," it must still guarantee fair procedures, including principles of natural justice. Certain rights related to human values may be protected by fundamental rights, even if not explicitly stated in the Constitution. For example, freedom of the press is covered under Article 19(1)(a) despite not being explicitly mentioned. However, the right to life under Article 21 does not include the right to die, contrary to popular belief is not related to the Maneka Gandhi Case. Hence, the correct option is d.

q: Who can be appointed as a Judge of the Supreme Court?
o: An advocate who has practised for at least ten years in any court
A Judge of a High Court for at least three years
A distinguished jurist in the opinion of the President of India
A person who has held a judicial office for at least fifteen years
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Supreme Court of India Explanation: The judges of the Supreme Court are appointed by the president. A person to be appointed as a judge of the Supreme Court should have the following qualifications: 1. He should be a citizen of India. 2. (a) He should have been a judge of a High Court (or high courts in succession) for five years; or (b) He should have been an advocate of a High Court (or High Courts in succession) for ten years; or (c) He should be a distinguished jurist in the opinion of the president. The Constitution has not prescribed a minimum age for appointment as a judge of the Supreme Court. Hence, the correct answer is c.

q: Which one of the following statements about the Supreme Court is not correct?
o: Under Article 129 and Article 144 of the Constitution of India, the Supreme Court has been vested with power to punish for contempt of court.
The Supreme Court has been vested with advisory jurisdiction.
The Supreme Court may refuse to provide opinion to the President, if so asked, in certain circumstances.
Law declared by the Supreme Court is binding on all courts within the territory of India.
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Supreme Court of India Explanation: Article 129 Constitution of India states that the Supreme Court to be a court of record. The Supreme Court shall be a court of record and shall have all the powers of such a court including the power to punish for contempt of itself. Article 144 of the Constitution mandates that “all authorities civil and judicial, in the territory of India shall act in aid of the Supreme Court. Hence, there is no mention of the Contempt of court. The judgements, proceedings and acts of the Supreme Court are recorded for perpetual memory and testimony. These records are admitted to be of evidentiary value and cannot be questioned when produced before any court. They are recognised as legal precedents and legal references. Hence, the correct answer is a.

q: Who among the following fixes the date of election of the Speaker of the Lok Sabha?
o: The Secretary General of the Lok Sabha
The Deputy Speaker of the Lok Sabha
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Parliamment Explanation: The Speaker is chosen by newly elected Members of Parliament from the Lok Sabha. The Speaker is elected by the Lok Sabha from amongst its members. Whenever the office of the Speaker falls vacant, the Lok Sabha elects another member to fill the vacancy. The date of election of the Speaker is fixed by the President. If only one candidate is nominated for Speaker, no formal vote is conducted. A division (vote) is called for if there are multiple nominations. MPs vote for their candidate on a date fixed by the President. The winner of the election becomes the Speaker of the Lok Sabha until the next general election. Hence, the correct option is a.

q: Which one of the following powers of the Supreme Court is also conferred on a High Court?
o: Power of doing complete justice in any cause or matter pending before the court
Power to allow/refuse Special Leave Petitions
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: At present, there are 25 high courts in the country Explanation: A high court in India enjoys the following jurisdiction and powers: Original jurisdiction. Writ jurisdiction. Appellate jurisdiction. Supervisory jurisdiction. Control over subordinate courts. A court of record. Power of judicial review As a court of record, a high court has two powers: (a) The judgements, proceedings and acts of the high courts are recorded for perpetual memory and testimony. These records are admitted to be of evidentiary value and cannot be questioned when produced before any subordinate court. They are recognised as legal precedents and legal references. (b) It has power to punish for contempt of court, either with simple imprisonment or with fine or with both. Hence, the correct answer is c.

q: Which one of the following powers has not been conferred on the President of India?
o: To appoint the Prime Minister of India.
To exercise discretionary power in deciding questions related to the disqualification of a Member of Parliament.
To grant pardon to a person who has been punished by a courtmartial
To remove a Judge of the High Court
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: President of India Explanation: Article 75: Specifies the appointment of the Prime Minister by the President. Article 72: Grants the President the power to grant pardons, reprieves, etc., particularly for death sentences, but not independently of the government. The President can remove a High Court Judge upon the passing of a resolution by both houses of Parliament with a two-thirds majority. Article 102: States that any question regarding disqualification of a member of Parliament shall be referred to the President for a final decision. Before making a decision on disqualification, the President must obtain the opinion of the Election Commission, and act accordingly, indicating that such decisions do not solely rely on the discretion of the President of India. Hence, the correct option is b.

q: Which one of the following statements with regard to the Directive Principles of State Policy is not correct?
o: They are not enforceable in a court of law.
They can override all the fundamental rights.
They can be related to social and economic justice.
They can be related to giving free legal aid.
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Directive Principles of State Policy Explanation: The 'Directive Principles of State Policy' (DPSP) provide guidelines for governance in India. Found in Part IV (Articles 36-51) of the Constitution, they are not enforceable by courts but are fundamental for legislation. Inspired by the Irish Constitution, DPSP aim to promote social fairness, economic well-being, and a welfare state. Although non-justiciable, it's the State's duty to apply them in lawmaking (Article 37). Executive agencies and the judiciary are urged to consider DPSP, despite lack of legal enforceability. They can be related to giving free legal aid,social and economic justice. In Champak Dorairajan vs. the State of Madras, the Supreme Court ruled that DPSP cannot override Fundamental Rights (Part III). Consequently, Parliament adjusted certain Fundamental Rights to align with DPSP, emphasizing their subordination to Basic Rights. Hence, the correct option is b.

q: Which one of the following statements about Public Accounts Committee is correct?
o: The Committee consists of not more than 15 members from the Lok Sabha and 10 members from the Rajya Sabha.
The Finance Minister is an ex officio member of the Committee.
A member of the Committee is given a term for one year.
The term of the office of the members of the Committee from the Rajya Sabha is extended even when they retire from the Rajya Sabha.
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Parliamentary Committees Explanation: The Public Accounts Committee was set up first in 1921 under the provisions of the Government of India Act of 1919 and has since been in existence. At present, it consists of 22 members (15 from the Lok Sabha and 7 from the Rajya Sabha). The members are elected by the Parliament every year from amongst its members according to the principle of proportional representation by means of the single transferable vote. Thus, all parties get due representation in it. The term of office of the members is one year. A minister cannot be elected as a member of the committee. The chairman of the committee is appointed from amongst its members by the Speaker. Hence, the correct answer is c.

q: The Constitution of India contains no provision for the constitution of municipalities in every State for
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Panchayats and Municipalities Explanation: Article 243Q of the Constitution requires the creation of local governing bodies in every state. It specifies the establishment of Nagar Panchayats for transitional areas, transitioning from rural to urban. Municipal Councils are designated for smaller urban regions. Municipal Corporations are established for larger urban areas. However, Article 243Q does not mention provisions for Urban Panchayats. Hence,the correct option is d.

q: The Electoral College for electing the President of India does not include which of the following? 1. All elected members of the Rajya Sabha 2. All elected members of the Legislative Assemblies of States 3. All members of the Legislative Councils of States Select the correct answer using the code given below.
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: President Explanation: | | Defence The President of India, also known as the first citizen, is the head of state and a part of the Union Executive. His role and provisions are detailed in Articles 52-78 of the Constitution, including specific articles (52-62) . The President is elected indirectly by an electoral college rather than through a direct public vote. The electoral college comprises elected members of the Lok Sabha, Rajya Sabha, state legislative assemblies (excluding Legislative Councils), and legislative assemblies of union territories like Delhi and Puducherry. Not involved in the electoral college of President's election are- nominated members of the Rajya Sabha and state legislative assemblies, members of Legislative Councils in bicameral legislatures, and nominated members of union territories like Delhi and Puducherry. Once elected, the President holds office for a term of five years with the possibility of re-election, without any restriction on the number of terms. Hence, the correct option is c.

q: Consider the following quote : "On the 26th January, 1950, we are going to enter a life of contradictions. In politics we will have equality and in social and economic life we will have inequality." To whom among the following leaders is this quote attributed?
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Making of the Constitution Explanation: Dr. B.R. Ambedkar, in his concluding speech to the Constituent Assembly, voiced concern over the unequal status of Dalits. He remarked on the contradiction of India's future, where political equality would coexist with social and economic inequality. While acknowledging the principle of one person, one vote in politics, Dr. Ambedkar highlighted the ongoing denial of equality in social and economic spheres. He advocated for equality not only in political representation but also in social and economic aspects of life. Dr. Ambedkar emphasized the need to address these contradictions for a more just society. Hence,the correct option is c.

q: Which one of the following statements with regard to the Preamble to the Constitution of India is not correct?
o: The term 'Secular Democratic Republic' was not there in the Preamble originally.
The Preamble contains the basic structure of the Constitution of India.
By itself, the Preamble is not enforceable in a court of law.
The Supreme Court has held that the Preamble cannot be considered as an aid to the legal interpretation of the Constitution, where the language is ambiguous.
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Preamble of the Indian constitution Explanation: The Preamble of the Indian Constitution is based on Jawaharlal Nehru's Objectives Resolution, presented in the Constituent Assembly in 1946 and accepted in 1947. It outlines the foundational principles and sources of authority of the Constitution. It contains the basic structure of the Constitution of India. Adopted by the Constituent Assembly on November 26, 1949, it became effective on January 26, 1950, India's Republic Day. The 42nd Amendment altered India's description to a "sovereign, socialist secular democratic republic" from 'Secular Democratic Republic' and modified the phrase "unity of the nation" to "unity and integrity of the nation." The preamble's amendment is permissible under Article 368, but its basic structure remains unchangeable. While not legally enforceable, the Preamble serves as a guiding principle in interpreting the Constitution. The Supreme Court recognizes the Preamble as an aid in legal interpretation, especially in cases of ambiguity within the Constitution. Hence,the correct option is d.

q: Which one of the following statements about 'personal liberty' is not correct?
o: State does not have the authority to deprive any person within the territory of India of his/her personal liberty without any rational basis.
Basis of depriving a person of his/her personal liberty must be in accordance with procedures established by law.
Personal liberty can be secured by the judicial writ of Habeas Corpus.
The majority view of the Supreme Court in A. K. Gopalan vs. State of Madras case invented 'due process of law'.
subject: polity
exp: Key concept: Fundamental Rights Explanation: Article 21 of the constitution defines the Right to Life and Personal Liberty. It states that no individual can be deprived of life or personal liberty except by law. This places limitations on the state's power to infringe upon these rights. 'Habeas Corpus' is a legal mechanism used to free unlawfully detained individuals securing personal liberty. It requires the presentation of the detained person before a court to assess the legality of their detention. Article 21 of the Indian Constitution emphasizes the protection of life and personal liberty. The Supreme Court in A.K. Gopalan vs. State of Madras (1950) highlighted that Articles 19(1) and 21 are not mutually exclusive. Hence,it did not invent 'due process of law'. Hence, the correct option is d.

q: Which among the following is/are the function/functions of the Cabinet Secretariat? 1. To ensure interministerial coordination 2. Management of major crisis situations in the country Select the correct answer using the code given below.
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: Parliament Explanation: Cabinet is a smaller body than the council of ministers, consisting of 15 to 20 ministers. It includes the cabinet ministers only. Thus, it is a part of the council of ministers. It meets, as a body, frequently and usually once in a week to deliberate and take decisions regarding the transaction of government business. It is chief coordinator of Central administration and ensures the interministerial cooordination. It is the chief crisis manager and thus deals with all emergency situations. Hence, the correct answer is c.

q: What is the term of office of the Chairperson and Members of the National Human Rights Commission?
o: 5 years or up to 70 years of age
3 years or up to 70 years of age
5 years or up to 65 years of age
3 years or up to 65 years of age
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: The National Human Rights Commission is the watchdog of human rights in the country. Explanation: National human rights commission is a statutory body, established in 1993 under a legislation enacted by the Parliament, namely, the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993. In the original act, the chairman and the members hold office for a term of five years or until they attain the age of 70 years. However it was amended in 2019, which reduced the term to 3 years or until they attain the age of 70 years. They are eligible for re-appointment. After their tenure, the chairperson and members are not eligible for further employment under the Central or a state government. Hence, the correct answer is b.

q: Which of the following statements about the Committee on Public Undertakings is/are correct? 1. There are more members from the Rajya Sabha than the Lok Sabha in the Committee. 2. The Chairperson of the Committee is appointed by the Speaker of the Lok Sabha. Select the correct answer using the code given below.
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: Parliamentary Committees Explanation: Committee on Public Undertakings was created in 1964 on the recommendation of the Krishna Menon Committee. Originally, it had 15 members (10 from the Lok Sabha and 5 from the Rajya Sabha). However, in 1974, its membership was raised to 22 (15 from the Lok Sabha and 7 from the Rajya Sabha). The members of this committee are elected by the Parliament every year from amongst its own members according to the principle of proportional representation by means of a single transferable vote. The term of office of the members is one year. A minister cannot be elected as a member of the committee. The chairman of the committee is appointed by the Speaker from amongst its members who are drawn from the Lok Sabha only. Hence, the correct answer is b.

q: The Swaran Singh Committee recommended the insertion of which one of the following provisions into the Constitution of India?
o: Nomination of Anglo-Indian Members to the Lok Sabha
Impeachment of the President of India
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: The Fundamental Duties in the Indian Constitution are inspired by the Constitution of erstwhile USSR. Explanation: Article 51A in Part IV A of the constitution deals with a set of duties known as Fundamental duties. These were not the part of original constitution and were added by the 42nd amendment act in 1976 by the recommendation of Swaran Singh Committee. Hence, the correct answer is b.

q: Which one of the following is correct in respect of the appointment of District Judges?
o: They are appointed by the Chief Justice of the concerned High Court.
They are appointed by the President of India on the advice of the Chief Justice of the concerned High Court.
They are appointed by the Governor in consultation with the High Court of the concerned State.
They are appointed by the Supreme Court on the advice of the concerned High Court.
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: District Courts Explanation: The appointment, posting and promotion of district judges in a state are made by the governor of the state in consultation with the high court. A person to be appointed as district judge should have the following qualifications: (a) He should not already be in the service of the Central or the state government. (b) He should have been an advocate or a pleader for seven years. (c) He should be recommended by the high court for appointment. Hence, the correct answer is c.

q: Which of the following is/are source/ sources of Law in India? 1. Constitution of India 2. Statutes 3. Customary Law 4. Case Law Select the correct answer using the code given below.
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: Sources of law in India Explanation: The Constitution of India is the supreme law of the land and it provides a legal framework for the political process and defining fundamental political principles. The sources of law in India include the Constitution of India, Statutes, Customary Law, and Case Law. These sources provide the legal framework and precedents that govern the Indian legal system. According to Article 13 of the Constitution, all the laws which are made before the Constitution will be void as long as they are violating the provisions of the Fundamental Rights. It further states that only that part of the law which will be void which is against the provisions of the constitution and not the whole law itself. Hence, the correct answer is d.

q: The landmark case of D.C.Wadhwa vs. State of Bihar in the Supreme Court is related to which one of the following powers of the Governor?
o: To revise the emoluments and allowances of the MLAs
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: Important Judgments Explanation: The governor under Article 213 can promulgate an ordinance only when the legislative assembly (in case of a unicameral legislature) is not in session or (in case of a bicameral legislature) when both the Houses of the state legislature are not in session or when either of the two Houses of the state legislature is not in session. In the D.C. Wadhwa case of 1987, the Supreme court pointed out that between 1967–1981 the Governor of Bihar promulgated 256 ordinances and all these were kept in force for periods ranging from one to fourteen years by promulgation from time to time. The court ruled that successive repromulgation of ordinances with the same text without any attempt to get the bills passed by the assembly would amount to violation of the Constitution and the ordinance so repromulgated is liable to be struck down. It held that the exceptional power of law-making through ordinance cannot be used as a substitute for the legislative power of the state legislature. Hence, the correct answer is a.

q: Which of the following statements about the Right to Information Act, 2005 is/are correct? 1. There shall be not more than twelve (12) Information Commissioners. 2. The Chief Information Commissioner shall hold office for a term of five years. 3. The Chief Information Commissioner is eligible for reappointment. Select the correct answer using the code given below :
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: The Central Information Commission was established by the Central Government in 2005 through an Official Gazette Notification under the provisions of the Right to Information Act (2005). Explanation: The Commission consists of a Chief Information Commissioner and not more than ten Information Commissioners. They are appointed by the President on the recommendation of a committee consisting of the Prime Minister as Chairperson, the Leader of Opposition in the Lok Sabha and a Union Cabinet Minister nominated by the Prime Minister. The Chief Information Commissioner and an Information Commissioner shall hold office for such term as prescribed by the Central Government which is fixed for a period of three years from the date on which he enters upon his office or until they attain the age of 65 years (as of 2024). whichever is earlier In 2022, the term for CICs were 5 years or until they attain the age of 65 years. They are not eligible for reappointment. Hence, the correct answer is b.

q: Which among the following deals with Habeas Corpus?
o: State of Uttar Pradesh v. Raj Narain and Ors. (1975)
Additional District Magistrate, Jabalpur v. Shivakant Shukla (1976)
Mrs. Maneka Gandhi v. Union of India (1978)
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: Habeas Corpus is an order issued by the court to a person who has detained another person, to produce the body of the latter before it Explanation: ADM Jabalpur v. Shivkant Shukla was a landmark judgement of the Supreme Court of India pertaining to the suspension of Articles 21 and 226 of the Indian Constitution in the event of a National Emergency. This controversial judgment of P.N. Bhagwati, decreed during the emergency from 25 June 1975 to 21 March 1977, held that a person’s right to not be unlawfully detained (i.e. habeas corpus) can be suspended in the interest of the State. This judgment received a lot of criticism since it reduced the importance attached to Fundamental Rights under the Indian Constitution. Hence, the correct answer is b.

q: Which of the following statements about National Emergency is/are correct? 1. A Proclamation of Emergency may be made by the President only when the security of India or any part thereof is threatened by war or external aggression or armed rebellion. 2. The Government of India acquires power to give directions to a State on any matter. 3. The Proclamation of Emergency does not suspend the State legislature. 4. The Proclamation of Emergency can continue for a maximum period of six months at a time only if approved by resolutions of both the Houses of Parliament. Select the correct answer using the code given below :
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: The Emergency provisions are contained in Part XVIII of the Constitution, from Articles 352 to 360. Explanation: Under Article 352, the President can declare a national emergency when the security of India or a part of it is threatened by war or external aggression or armed rebellion. It may be noted that the president can declare a national emergency even before the actual occurrence of war or external aggression or armed rebellion, if he is satisfied that there is an imminent danger. The proclamation of Emergency must be approved by both the Houses of Parliament within one month from the date of its issue. If approved by both the Houses of Parliament, the emergency continues for six months, and can be extended to an indefinite period with an approval of the Parliament for every six months. Effects on Centre-State relations: During a national emergency, the executive power of the Centre extends to directing any state regarding the manner in which its executive power is to be exercised. During a national emergency, the Parliament becomes empowered to make laws on any subject mentioned in the State List. Although the legislative power of a state legislature is not suspended, it becomes subject to the overriding power of the Parliament. Hence, the correct answer is d.

q: Which among the following is not correct?
o: Legislation on criminal law, barring exceptions, is a subject under the Concurrent List.
Bankruptcy and Insolvency are subjects under the Concurrent List.
Inter-State trade and commerce is a subject under the State List.
Banking is exclusively under the Union List.
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: The Constitution provides for a three-fold distribution of legislative subjects between the Centre and the states, viz., List-I (the Union List), List-II (the State List) and List-III (the Concurrent List) in the Seventh Schedule. Explanation: The Parliament has exclusive powers to make laws with respect to any of the matters enumerated in the Union List. This list has at present 98 subjects (originally 97 subjects) like defence, banking, foreign affairs, currency, atomic energy, insurance, communication, inter- state trade and commerce, census, audit and so on. The state legislature has “in normal circumstances” exclusive powers to make laws with respect to any of the matters enumerated in the State List. This has at present 59 subjects (originally 662 subjects) like public order, police, public health and sanitation, agriculture, prisons, local government, fisheries, markets, theaters, gambling and so on. Both, the Parliament and state legislature can make laws with respect to any of the matters enumerated in the Concurrent List. This list has at present 52 subjects (originally 473 subjects) like criminal law and procedure, civil procedure, marriage and divorce, population control and family planning, electricity, labour welfare, economic and social planning, drugs, newspapers, books and printing press, and others. Hence, the correct answer is c.

q: The National Human Rights Commission was established under the statute of :
o: The Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993
The Protection and Implementation of Human Rights Act, 1993
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: The National Human Rights Commission is a watchdog of human rights in India. Explanation: The National Human Rights Commission is a statutory (and not a constitutional) body. It was established in 1993 under legislation enacted by the Parliament, namely, the Protection of Human Rights Act, 1993. The commission is a multi-member body consisting of a chairperson and five members. Its major objectives is: To strengthen the institutional arrangements through which human rights issues could be addressed in their entirety in a more focussed manner. To look into allegations of excesses, independently of the government, in a manner that would underline the government’s commitment to protect human rights To complement and strengthen the efforts that have already been made in this direction Hence, the correct answer is a.

q: Who among the following was the first pro- tem Speaker to be unanimously elected as the Speaker of the Lok Sabha?
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: Each House of Parliament has its own presiding officer. There is a Speaker and a Deputy Speaker for the Lok Sabha. Explanation: Veteran parliamentarian and CPM leader Somnath Chatterjee was unanimously elected Speaker of the 14th Lok Sabha (17 May 2004 – 18 May 2009). Mr Chatterjee, a 10-time MP from Bolpur constituency in West Bengal, became the first Communist to occupy this high office. He also became the second Pro -Tem Speaker to be elected as the Speaker of the Lower House. V Mavalankar was the first Pro-Tem Speaker to become the presiding officer in Lok Sabha in 1952. Hence, the correct answer is c.

q: Which among the following statements regarding the powers of the High Courts under Article 226 of the Constitution of India is not correct?
o: It can issue writs of habeas corpus, mandamus, quo warranto, certiorari, and prohibition.
Writs can be issued to enforce any rights conferred by Part-III and for any other purpose.
This power can derogate the power conferred on the Supreme Court under Article 32(2).
Writ can be issued to any authority under its jurisdiction.
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: Writs are a written order from the Supreme Court or High Court that commands constitutional remedies for Indian citizens against the violation of their fundamental rights or for any other purposes. Explanation: The Supreme Court (under Article 32) and the high courts (under Article 226) can issue the writs of habeas corpus, mandamus, prohibition, certiorari and quo-warranto. A high court can issue writs against a person residing or against a government or authority located within its territorial jurisdiction only or outside its territorial jurisdiction only if the cause of action arises within its territorial jurisdiction. A high court can issue writs both for the enforcement of Fundamental Rights in part III and also for any other purpose. The power conferred on a High Court by this article shall not be in derogation of the power conferred on the Supreme Court by clause (2) of article 32. Hence, the correct answer is c.

q: The Judgment of the Supreme Court in Peoples' Union for Civil Liberties . Union of India 2004 is related to which of the following?
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: The right of information is a fundamental right under Article 19(1)(a) of the Constitution. Explanation: In The People's Union for Civil Liberties, the Supreme Court of India ruled that Indian citizens have the right, according to Article 19(1)(a) of the Indian Constitution, to access information about political candidates. The Court also observed that “the right to hold a telephone conversation in the privacy of one's home or office without interference can certainly be claimed as 'right to privacy' and held that telephone-tapping would violate Article 21 unless it was permitted under a “procedure established by law”. Hence, the correct answer is c.

q: Under which one among the following Articles of the Constitution of India, a member of a Civil Service, whether of the Union or of a State, seeks protection from unlawful dismissal from service?
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: Important Articles Explanation: The Jammu and Kashmir Lieutenant-Governor recently terminated the employment of six government personnel by using Article 311 of the Constitution. About Article 311: Article 311 of the Constitution: “Dismissal, removal or reduction in rank of persons employed in civil capacities under the Union or a State.– (1) No person who is a member of a civil service of the Union or an all-India service or a civil service of a State or holds a civil post under the Union or a State shall be dismissed or removed by an authority subordinate to that by which he was appointed. (2) No civil servant shall be dismissed or removed or reduced in rank except after an inquiry in which s/he has been informed of the charges and given a reasonable opportunity of being heard in respect of those charges. The members of Civil service of the Union, All India Service, and Civil service of any State are protected under Article 311. People who hold a civil post under the Union or any State. Article 311 is applicable only to civil servants, i.e. public officers. They are not available to defence personnel. Hence, the correct answer is b.

q: Who among the following Presidents of India, sent back the Post Office (Amendment) Bill, 1986 for the reconsideration of the Parliament?
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: President Explanation: The President of India who sent back the Indian Post Office (Amendment) Bill, 1986 for reconsideration was Giani Zail Singh. He exercised a pocket veto on this bill, which was passed by both Houses of Parliament but not assented to by him. Instead of rejecting or returning it, he chose to keep it pending without action until the end of his term in 1987. Following his departure from office, President R. Venkataraman eventually returned the bill to Parliament in January 1990 for further consideration. The President has no veto power in respect of a constitutional amendment bill. The 24th Constitutional Amendment Act of 1971 made it obligatory for the President to give his assent to a constitutional amendment bill. Hence, the correct answer is c.

q: Which of the following statements is/are correct? 1. Some Rights contained in Part-III of the Constitution of India are enforceable against the State only 2. Some Rights contained in Part-III of the Constitution of India are enforceable in favor of citizens only 3. Some Rights contained in Part-III of the Constitution of India are enforceable against both, the state and individuals Select the answer using the code given below :
subject: polity
exp: Key Concept: Fundamental Rights Explanation: The Fundamental Rights are enshrined in Part III of the Constitution from Articles 12 to 35. The Fundamental Rights guaranteed by the Constitution are characterised by the following: Some of them are available only to the citizens while others are available to all persons whether citizens, foreigners or legal persons like corporations or companies Fundamental Rights primarily impose restrictions on the State, ensuring that it does not infringe upon these rights. Fundamental Rights are specifically designed to protect the rights of citizens against violations by the State. All of them are available against the arbitrary action of the state. However, some of them are also available against the action of private individuals Right to equality: Articles 14–18 of the Constitution guarantee equality before law, prohibit discrimination, and ensure equality of opportunity in employment. Right to freedom: Articles 19–22 of the Constitution guarantee freedom of speech and expression, assembly, association, movement, and residence. Right to freedom of religion: Articles 25–28 of the Constitution guarantee freedom of conscience and the practice of religion. Right to constitutional remedies: Articles 32 of the Constitution provide for constitutional remedies to enforce fundamental rights. These fundamental rights are enforceable by the courts, subject to certain restrictions.
